questions.stem: Spotify's 'Discover Weekly' playlist is primarily powered by which type of AI?
options: Computer vision
Recommendation system (collaborative filtering + content analysis)
Speech recognition
Reinforcement learning
questions.explanation: Discover Weekly uses collaborative filtering (users who listen to similar songs as you) combined with audio analysis features. It's a recommendation system — a core ML application type.

questions.stem: What is the 'cold start' problem in recommendation systems?
options: The system is slow to start up
New users or items have no interaction history, making it hard to generate recommendations
The recommendation model requires cold storage
Recommendations degrade in cold weather
questions.explanation: New users have no watch/purchase history, so collaborative filtering can't find similar users. New items have no ratings. The cold start problem requires alternative strategies like asking preferences or using demographic data.

questions.stem: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) solves which key limitation of LLMs?
options: LLMs can't generate text
LLMs have a fixed knowledge cutoff and can hallucinate when asked about specific facts
LLMs require too much compute
LLMs can only answer yes/no questions
questions.explanation: LLMs are trained at a point in time and may confabulate facts. RAG retrieves relevant documents at query time and grounds the answer in them, allowing LLMs to answer about up-to-date or domain-specific information.

questions.stem: Object detection (e.g. YOLO) differs from image classification because:
options: Detection is faster than classification
Detection identifies what objects are present AND where they are (bounding boxes)
Detection only works on photos, classification works on any image
Detection doesn't use neural networks
questions.explanation: Classification assigns a single label to an image. Detection localises multiple objects with bounding boxes and classifies each one. Detection is much harder and used in applications like self-driving cars and security cameras.

questions.stem: A two-stage recommendation system uses a retrieval stage and a ranking stage because:
options: One model isn't powerful enough
The retrieval stage quickly narrows millions of items to ~1000 candidates; the ranking stage precisely scores those candidates
Rules require two models by law
The two stages are trained on different datasets
questions.explanation: Scoring all millions of items with an expensive model in real-time is impossible. Fast retrieval (approximate nearest neighbours) narrows the space. A heavier, more accurate ranker then picks the best from this smaller candidate set.

questions.stem: Constitutional AI (developed by Anthropic) differs from standard RLHF in that:
options: It uses more human raters
It doesn't use any human feedback
Instead of human comparisons alone, it uses a set of principles (constitution) for the model to critique and revise its own outputs
It trains only on code
questions.explanation: Standard RLHF requires large-scale human preference labelling. Constitutional AI uses a written constitution of principles — the model critiques its own responses against these principles, reducing reliance on human labelling at scale.
